title: The Springfield Files

jokes.text: Hello. I'm Leonard Nimoy. The following tale of alien encounters is true. And by true, I mean false. It's all lies. But they're entertaining lies. And in the end, isn't that the real truth? The answer is no.
jokes.setup: Host introduces story as true alien encounter tale
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicts himself, then undermines his own philosophical question
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-commentary on TV narratives and philosophical pretension. Perfect escalation from contradiction to pseudo-profound to deflating reality check.

dialogue.speaker: Leonard Nimoy
dialogue.line: This is a true story of an alien encounter.

dialogue.speaker: Leonard Nimoy
dialogue.line: Or is it?

dialogue.speaker: Leonard Nimoy
dialogue.line: No, it is.

jokes.text: I saw this in a movie about a bus that had to speed around the city... keeping its speed over 50. And if its speed dropped, it would explode. I think it was called The Bus that Couldn't Slow Down.
jokes.setup: Homer describes the plot of Speed accurately
jokes.punchline: Completely butchers the title in the most literal way possible
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer character beat - shows his literal mindset and cultural obliviousness. The 'title' became a classic Simpsons reference.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So there's this bus, right? And there's a bomb on it, and if the bus slows down below 50 miles per hour, the bomb goes off. So they gotta keep it going fast the whole time.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's called 'A Bus That Had to Keep Going or It Would Blow Up.'

jokes.text: Homer sets up VCR loop to fake working at the power plant
jokes.setup: Homer needs to skip work to drink
jokes.punchline: Elaborate scheme using old footage on loop
jokes.explanation: Solid visual gag showing Homer's lazy ingenuity. Well-executed but not particularly original concept.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I need a day off to drink. I know! I'll set up old footage of me working on a loop so Mr. Burns won't notice I'm gone.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: This is foolproof! By the time he figures it out, I'll be three sheets to the wind at Moe's.

jokes.text: Something gay, no doubt? - What? What? You know, lighthearted, fancy-free. Mothers, lock up your daughters. Smithers is on the town.
jokes.setup: Burns asks what Smithers will do Friday night, uses 'gay' in old-fashioned sense
jokes.punchline: Smithers' panic followed by Burns' oblivious heterosexual interpretation
jokes.explanation: Classic Burns obliviousness to Smithers' sexuality. Good character work with wordplay on 'gay' meanings.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Smithers, what are you doing this Friday night? I hope you'll be gay!

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: G-gay, sir?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Yes, gay! Happy! Don't you know what gay means?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Oh! Oh yes, sir. Happy. I'll be very happy.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Good. I'm taking you to the Boyz II Men concert.

jokes.text: Thank God it's Wednesday! - It's Friday. - Uh-oh. Wrong pills.
jokes.setup: Grandpa celebrates wrong day
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's taken wrong medication
jokes.explanation: Good character beat for senile Grandpa. The 'wrong pills' explanation is both logical and absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa Simpson
dialogue.line: Today's a great day! I'm celebrating my birthday!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dad, your birthday was three months ago.

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, I feel fantastic! Must be this new medication the doctor gave me.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: What kind of medication, Dad?

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa Simpson
dialogue.line: I don't know, but the bottle says 'Take one daily.' So I took thirty!

jokes.text: Sorry, Donkey Kong. You are just not a draw anymore. - Hey, he's still got it!
jokes.setup: Worker apologizes to unpopular Donkey Kong arcade machine
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately proves him wrong by playing enthusiastically
jokes.explanation: Nice satire of arcade culture and Homer's childlike enthusiasm perfectly timed to contradict the worker's statement.

dialogue.speaker: Arcade Worker
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, nobody likes you anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh! Ooh! Ah! Hoo-hoo! Yes! Come on, you bucket of bolts!

jokes.text: Game over. Please deposit 40 quarters. - What a rip!
jokes.setup: Homer counts out exactly 40 quarters
jokes.punchline: Machine immediately demands 40 more quarters, Homer's outrage
jokes.explanation: Solid observation about arcade machine greed, but fairly predictable setup and payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Forty quarters! That's ten dollars!

dialogue.speaker: Arcade Machine
dialogue.line: Insert 40 more quarters.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Friday's just another day between NBC's Must-See Thursday and CBS's Saturday night crap-o-rama.
jokes.setup: Lisa philosophizes about Friday's meaninglessness
jokes.punchline: Reduces it to TV scheduling terms, calls CBS programming 'crap-o-rama'
jokes.explanation: Smart media satire. 'Crap-o-rama' is a perfect Lisa coinage mixing sophistication with crude assessment.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Friday is just an arbitrary social construct with no real meaning. It's merely a human invention to organize our perception of time.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But if you really think about it, Friday only matters because of television scheduling. Networks decide what gets aired, and that dictates our entire culture.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: CBS's Friday night programming is nothing but a crap-o-rama of mindless drivel designed to keep the masses sedated.

jokes.text: Düff, from Sweden. - Wait a minute! This is Duff! - Ya got me, didn't ya?
jokes.setup: Moe presents 'foreign' beer with fancy pronunciation
jokes.punchline: It's just regular Duff with an umlaut, Moe admits the scam
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for scheming Moe. The umlaut gag is simple but effective satire of beer marketing.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Ladies and gentlemen, I present to you a new imported beer from the exotic land of... Düff!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Düff? That sounds fancy!

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: You bet it is. It's brewed in the mountains of... uh... Duffland.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'll take a case!

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Alright, alright, I'll level with ya. It's just regular Duff with an umlaut over the 'u'.

jokes.text: Bold, refreshing... and something I can't quite put my finger on. [Dogs Barking] Hmm. Needs more dog.
jokes.setup: Homer tastes mysterious beer and analyzes its flavor
jokes.punchline: Reveals the mystery ingredient is dog, wants more of it
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark and absurd. The delayed reveal with dog barking is perfectly timed, and Homer's casual acceptance is disturbing and hilarious.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm... notes of... wheat, hops, and... what is this? There's something else here. A certain... je ne sais quoi.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: That's dog, Homer. You're drinking dog.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dog? Why didn't you say so? Mmm, dog beer!

jokes.text: Better go home and spend some quality time with the kids.
jokes.setup: Homer decides to be a responsible father at 1 AM
jokes.punchline: The irony of 'quality time' when kids are asleep
jokes.explanation: Solid character moment showing Homer's obliviousness to proper parenting timing, but not particularly memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm going to be a responsible father and spend quality time with my kids.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: 1 AM is the perfect time!

jokes.text: Uh, I guess I'll walk home.
jokes.setup: Breathalyzer test presumably fails
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual acceptance of walking home extremely drunk
jokes.explanation: Basic setup for the alien encounter. Homer's casual acceptance of being too drunk to drive is mildly amusing but predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Police Officer
dialogue.line: You're way over the limit. You're not driving anywhere.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's okay, I'll just walk.

jokes.text: Oh, Son, I'm glad to see ya! I went for the morning paper and I got lost! - No time for you, old man.
jokes.setup: Grandpa pathetically lost and seeking help
jokes.punchline: Homer coldly dismisses his own father
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's selfishness and Grandpa's helplessness. The cruelty is both funny and uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Grandpa Simpson
dialogue.line: Help me, I'm lost!

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Sorry, Dad, I can't help you right now.

jokes.text: Have you been drinking? - No! Well, 10 beers.
jokes.setup: Marge questions Homer's sobriety
jokes.punchline: Homer denies drinking then immediately contradicts himself
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer logic failure. The immediate contradiction and casual admission of heavy drinking is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, have you been drinking?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No! I haven't had a single drop... well, maybe just a Duff or two.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, a six-pack. But that's it!

jokes.text: When I came to, I was covered with a sticky translucent goo. - More sausage?
jokes.setup: Homer describes disturbing alien encounter aftermath
jokes.punchline: Marge offers breakfast sausage at worst possible moment
jokes.explanation: Excellent uncomfortable comedy. The timing of offering sausage during gross goo description is perfectly awkward.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It was horrible, Marge. They did things to me... unspeakable things. I can never be the same.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh Homer, you'll feel better after some of my breakfast sausage.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge!

jokes.text: According to Junior Skeptic Magazine, the chances are a 175 million-to-one... So? It's just the people who claim they've seen aliens are always pathetic lowlifes with boring jobs. Oh. And you, Dad.
jokes.setup: Lisa explains statistical improbability and typical alien witness profile
jokes.punchline: Realizes description perfectly matches her father, adds him as afterthought
jokes.explanation: Brilliant burn from Lisa. The delayed realization and casual addition of 'And you, Dad' is perfectly cruel and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: According to the FBI, the average alien witness is a white male, age 40 to 50, with a high school education and poor social skills.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: The statistical probability of such a person actually encountering an alien is approximately one in a billion.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Oh my God... Dad!

jokes.text: I am the thing from Uranus! - Oh, it's Bart. I can't believe it. I'm being mocked by my own children on my birthday.
jokes.setup: Bart pranks Homer with alien costume
jokes.punchline: Homer's pathetic self-pity about birthday mistreatment
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's persecution complex. The birthday detail adds pathos to the mockery.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why does everyone always forget my birthday? I slave away at that nuclear plant, come home, and what do I get? A prank from my own son in some ridiculous alien costume!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Nobody cares about Homer Simpson. Not my wife, not my kids, not even Lenny and Carl would remember if it wasn't for free donuts.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm pathetic. Absolutely pathetic.

jokes.text: It's your birthday? - Yes. Remember, it's the same day as the dog's.
jokes.setup: Family forgot Homer's birthday
jokes.punchline: Homer shares birthday with dog, family remembers dog but not Homer
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup for next joke. Shows how little the family thinks of Homer compared to their pet.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, I share my birthday with Santa's Little Helper.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh, that's nice, Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, so when Santa's Little Helper's birthday comes around, we're gonna throw him a big party.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Cool!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's great, Santa's Little Helper.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait a minute... nobody remembered MY birthday.

jokes.text: Santa's Little Helper, it's your birthday? Oh. We gotta get you a present. Yes, we do. Yes, we do. We love you, boy. Good doggy. Good doggy. - Lousy lovable dog.
jokes.setup: Family discovers it's the dog's birthday
jokes.punchline: Everyone lavishes attention on dog while ignoring Homer's identical birthday
jokes.explanation: Excellent payoff to setup. Homer's muttered complaint about the 'lousy lovable dog' is perfect defeated jealousy.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh my God, it's Santa's Little Helper's birthday!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: We have to celebrate! He's part of the family!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Yeah! Let's get him a cake and presents!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. Today's my birthday too.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That's nice, Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Nice? It's the same day as the dog's! We could celebrate together!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: But Dad, Santa's Little Helper is so special!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: He's a good boy!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm a good boy! Aren't I? Aren't I?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Of course, Homer. Now come on, kids, let's make a birthday cake for the dog!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What about my cake?

dialogue.speaker: Family
dialogue.line: Woof!

jokes.text: Oh, it was awful. They set me on a cold, metal table and prodded me with humiliating probes. And then- Oh, wait. That was my physical.
jokes.setup: Homer describes traumatic alien abduction experience
jokes.punchline: Realizes he's describing routine medical exam
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection joke. Plays on alien abduction tropes while making a point about uncomfortable medical procedures.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: They took me up into the ship, and started doing things to me. Horrible things. They put this long, cold thing inside me. Over and over again. I was begging them to stop, but they wouldn't listen!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's your yearly physical!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, that explains the cookies they gave me at the end.

jokes.text: The alien has a sweet, heavenly voice, like Urkel. And he appears every Friday night... like Urkel!
jokes.setup: Homer describes alien's voice and schedule
jokes.punchline: Compares everything to Steve Urkel for no logical reason
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor showing Homer's pop culture-addled brain. The repetition of 'like Urkel' is perfectly illogical.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The alien had this weird voice, kept saying 'Did I do that?' and showed up every Tuesday at 3 PM like clockwork.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, it was just like Steve Urkel!

jokes.text: Well, your story is very compelling, Mr. Jackass- I mean, Simpson. So, I'll just type it up on my invisible typewriter.
jokes.setup: Wiggum pretends to take Homer seriously
jokes.punchline: Calls him jackass, then mimes typing on invisible typewriter
jokes.explanation: Great character moment for sarcastic Wiggum. The invisible typewriter bit is childishly perfect mockery.

dialogue.speaker: Police Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Calls him jackass, then mimes typing on invisible typewriter

jokes.text: I just torched a building downtown and I'm afraid I'll do it again! - Yeah, right. I'll just type it up on my invisible typewriter.
jokes.setup: Arsonist confesses to serious crime
jokes.punchline: Wiggum dismisses real crime with same sarcasm he used for Homer
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback showing Wiggum's incompetence. He dismisses actual crime with same mockery used for Homer's story.

dialogue.speaker: Arsonist
dialogue.line: Chief, I've been setting fires all over town. I've burned down the school, the hospital, the retirement home...

dialogue.speaker: Chief Wiggum
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah, and I suppose you're gonna tell me you also ate all the donuts in the evidence locker.

jokes.text: There's also this report of a shipment of drugs and illegal weapons coming into New Jersey tonight. - I hardly think the F.B.I.'s concerned with matters like that.
jokes.setup: Scully suggests investigating actual crime
jokes.punchline: Mulder dismisses drug/weapon smuggling as unimportant to FBI
jokes.explanation: Perfect X-Files parody. Shows Mulder's obsession with paranormal over actual law enforcement priorities.

jokes.text: Is this about that pen that I took from the post office? I swear, I didn't know I put it in my purse! Then I was going to bring it back, but the dog chewed it up. And that just made things worse.
jokes.setup: FBI agents arrive at door
jokes.punchline: Marge panics and over-confesses to minor pen theft
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge character moment. Her guilt-ridden over-confession about trivial pen theft shows her neurotic honesty.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh my God, the FBI! Um, yes, I took a pen from the bank in 1987. And I used the grocery store scale without buying anything. I also once kept a library book for... for three weeks overdue!

jokes.text: [Electrical shock sounds] Ow.
jokes.setup: Homer hooked up to lie detector
jokes.punchline: Gets shocked for unknown reason (presumably lying)
jokes.explanation: Basic slapstick gag. Sets up the weight loss 'test' but isn't particularly clever on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I have never been unfaithful to Marge.

dialogue.speaker: Examiner
dialogue.line: That's a lie.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What?! No it isn't!

dialogue.speaker: Examiner
dialogue.line: The machine says otherwise.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well your machine is wrong!

dialogue.speaker: Examiner
dialogue.line: Homer, the machine is never wrong.

jokes.text: What's the point of this test? - No point. I just thought he could stand to lose a little weight. His jiggling is almost hypnotic. - Yes. It's like a lava lamp.
jokes.setup: Mulder questions the lie detector test
jokes.punchline: Scully reveals it's just to make Homer lose weight, both enjoy watching him jiggle
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor. The casual cruelty and lava lamp comparison is both disturbing and hilarious. Excellent character subversion.

dialogue.speaker: Mulder
dialogue.line: This lie detector test seems highly irregular.

dialogue.speaker: Scully
dialogue.line: It's not real. We just tell Homer it is so he'll lose weight.

dialogue.speaker: Mulder
dialogue.line: Why would that work?

dialogue.speaker: Scully
dialogue.line: He thinks the machine can detect lies. So he tries to stay calm and composed.

dialogue.speaker: Mulder
dialogue.line: And does he lose weight?

dialogue.speaker: Scully
dialogue.line: No. But watching him jiggle when he gets nervous is hilarious.

dialogue.speaker: Mulder
dialogue.line: Ha. Yes.

jokes.text: Well, the evening began at the gentlemen's club, where we were discussing Wittgenstein over a game of backgammon. - Mr. Simpson, it's a felony to lie to the F.B.I. - We were sitting in Barney's car eating packets of mustard. You happy?
jokes.setup: Homer fabricates sophisticated evening activities
jokes.punchline: When threatened, admits pathetic reality of eating mustard packets
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment. The contrast between pretentious lie and pathetic reality is beautifully executed. Eating mustard packets is perfectly sad.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I've been taking advanced courses at the community college. Tonight I'm attending a lecture on 19th-century French philosophy.

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.line: Is that so? And what exactly were you doing last Tuesday evening?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I... I was at the ballet. Yes, the ballet.

dialogue.speaker: Agent
dialogue.line: Mr. Simpson, we have evidence that contradicts your story.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, you got me. I was home eating mustard packets straight from the condiment drawer.

jokes.text: You are one fine lookin' woman, lady. If I wasn't married, I'd go out with you like that!
jokes.setup: Drunk Barney hits on Agent Scully
jokes.punchline: Crude drunken compliment with weird conditional logic
jokes.explanation: Good Barney character moment showing his drunk crudeness. The 'if I wasn't married' qualifier is absurd given the context.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Hey there, gorgeous. If you were a door, I'd slam you all night long. And if you were a window, I'd... I'd look through you. Real slow-like.

jokes.text: I am so sorry. Whatever you do, don't tell Marge. God, I love her!
jokes.setup: Homer apologizes for Barney's behavior
jokes.punchline: Asks agents not to tell his wife, expresses genuine love
jokes.explanation: Sweet character moment showing Homer's genuine love for Marge despite his flaws. Good emotional beat.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm sorry about Barney. He's usually not like this.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Please, don't tell Marge about this. She means everything to me. I really do love her.

jokes.text: All right, they're on to us! Get him back to Sea World!
jokes.setup: FBI agents mention their identities
jokes.punchline: Someone panics about returning someone to Sea World
jokes.explanation: Random absurdist gag implying some Sea World creature is being hidden. The panic and specificity make it funny.

dialogue.speaker: FBI Agent
dialogue.line: We're with the FBI.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh no! Does this mean I have to return Flipper to Sea World?

dialogue.speaker: FBI Agent
dialogue.line: What? No, we just need to ask you some questions.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, thank God. Because I've grown very attached to that dolphin.

jokes.text: So I says, blue M&M, red M&M- they all wind up the same color in the end.
jokes.setup: Barney philosophizes about M&M colors
jokes.punchline: Dark observation that they're all the same color when digested
jokes.explanation: Good drunk philosophy from Barney. The observation is both gross and oddly profound, typical of drunk wisdom.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: You know, it's funny... all these different colors of M&Ms, red, green, blue... but when you digest 'em, they're all the same color.

jokes.text: For the love of God, help me! I've been here for four days and a turtle's got ahold of my teeth!
jokes.setup: Grampa emerges from woods
jokes.punchline: Bizarre specific complaint about turtle dental theft
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd and specific. The image of a turtle stealing dentures is both ridiculous and somehow believable for Grampa.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Those dang turtles stole my teeth again! And this time they got my denture adhesive too!

jokes.text: Ow! He bit me with my own teeth! - This is the worst assignment we've ever had. - Worse than the time we were attacked by the flesh-eating virus?
jokes.setup: Grampa attacks with his own teeth that turtle had
jokes.punchline: Agents compare this to being attacked by flesh-eating virus
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation and X-Files parody. The casual mention of flesh-eating virus as previous assignment is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Mulder
dialogue.line: This is consistent with a flesh-eating virus.

dialogue.speaker: Scully
dialogue.line: Mulder, it was just an old man's dentures.

jokes.text: But somewhere out there, something is watching us. There are alien forces acting in ways we can't perceive. Are we alone in the universe? Impossible when you consider the wonders that exist all around us. Voodoo priests of Haiti, the Tibetan numerologists of Appalachia, the unsolved mysteries of Unsolved Mysteries.
jokes.setup: Mulder delivers typical X-Files monologue about mysteries
jokes.punchline: Includes 'Tibetan numerologists of Appalachia' and 'unsolved mysteries of Unsolved Mysteries'
jokes.explanation: Great X-Files parody. 'Tibetan numerologists of Appalachia' is wonderfully nonsensical, and the Unsolved Mysteries reference is meta-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Mulder
dialogue.line: The truth is out there, hidden among the Tibetan numerologists of Appalachia and the unsolved mysteries of Unsolved Mysteries.

jokes.text: Who'd have thought a whale could be so heavy? - Geez, it's the feds!
jokes.setup: Workers struggling with heavy object
jokes.punchline: Reveals it's a whale, panic about federal agents
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier Sea World gag. The obvious observation about whale weight adds to the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Sea World Worker 1
dialogue.line: Come on, heave! One more time!

dialogue.speaker: Sea World Worker 2
dialogue.line: I got it, I got it...

dialogue.speaker: Sea World Worker 1
dialogue.line: What is this thing anyway?

dialogue.speaker: Sea World Worker 2
dialogue.line: I don't know, it's huge!

dialogue.speaker: Sea World Worker 1
dialogue.line: Wait... oh no...

dialogue.speaker: Sea World Worker 2
dialogue.line: What? WHAT?!

dialogue.speaker: Sea World Worker 1
dialogue.line: It's a whale.

dialogue.speaker: Sea World Worker 2
dialogue.line: A whale?! We're gonna lose our jobs!

dialogue.speaker: Sea World Worker 1
dialogue.line: The feds are gonna shut us down!

dialogue.speaker: Sea World Worker 2
dialogue.line: Quick, hide it before anyone sees!

jokes.text: I don't believe you, Homer. - You do? Oh, Marge! You've made me so happy. - You're not listening. You're only hearing what you want to hear. - Thanks! I'd love an omelet right about now.
jokes.setup: Marge clearly states she doesn't believe Homer
jokes.punchline: Homer hears what he wants, then mishears criticism as omelet offer
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer selective hearing gag. The escalation from misunderstanding to complete non-sequitur is beautifully executed.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, I don't believe you.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You said you'd make me an omelet!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I said no such thing.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But you said 'omelet'...

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I said you're a complete idiot.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm... complete idiot omelet.

jokes.text: I'm like the man who single-handedly built the rocket and went to the moon. What was his name? Apollo Creed?
jokes.setup: Homer makes grandiose comparison to space pioneer
jokes.punchline: Confuses astronaut program with Rocky character
jokes.explanation: Good Homer knowledge failure. The confusion of Apollo program with Apollo Creed is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm like a space pioneer, boldly going where no man has gone before.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's from Star Trek.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, I'm thinking of Rocky. You know, Apollo Creed?

jokes.text: Unless you're feeling amorous. [Growls] - No, I'm not. - Well, then, good night.
jokes.setup: Homer refuses to share bed unless Marge wants romance
jokes.punchline: Immediate acceptance when she says no
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's mix of stubbornness and practical acceptance. The timing is well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm not sharing this bed unless you want romance.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Deal!

jokes.text: Thank you, Son. Now, do you think you could stop the casual swearing? - Hell, yes!
jokes.setup: Homer asks Bart to stop swearing
jokes.punchline: Bart swears while agreeing to stop swearing
jokes.explanation: Classic contradiction gag. Simple but well-executed with perfect timing and character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Bart, I want you to stop swearing.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Okay, Dad. I won't say any more f***ing bad words.

jokes.text: What if we don't? - We'll fake it and sell it to the Fox network. They'll buy anything. - Now, Son, they do a lot of quality programming too. [Pause] I kill me.
jokes.setup: Homer suggests selling fake footage to Fox
jokes.punchline: Defends Fox then immediately contradicts himself with pride
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-humor. The show mocking its own network while Homer's self-satisfied 'I kill me' is perfectly delivered. Became a classic quote.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We could sell fake footage to Fox!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: But Dad, Fox is a respectable news organization.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You're right, you're right. Fox would never air fake footage.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm so proud we're selling it to them!

jokes.text: Now, Son, they do a lot of quality programming too. [Pause] I kill me.
jokes.setup: Homer defends Fox's quality
jokes.punchline: Admits he was being sarcastic
jokes.explanation: Great follow-up to network joke. Homer's self-awareness is perfectly timed.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Fox has the highest quality programming on television.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Just kidding, I was being sarcastic.

jokes.text: Bud. - Weis. - Er. [Pause] Coors.
jokes.setup: Father and son spell out 'Budweiser' together
jokes.punchline: Homer breaks pattern by saying 'Coors' instead
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection gag. Sets up expectation of brand loyalty then subverts it with competitor mention.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: B

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: U

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: W

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: E

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: S

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: E

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: R

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Coors

jokes.text: And that is how much college will cost for Maggie. [Screaming] No! No! No!
jokes.setup: Homer tells ghost story
jokes.punchline: Reveals it was about college costs, everyone screams in horror
jokes.explanation: Clever subversion of ghost story expectation. College costs as horror story is relatable and well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: So there I was, walking through the woods at midnight, when suddenly I heard this terrifying sound...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A voice whispered, 'Your child's college tuition will cost $200,000.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And the interest rates keep compounding every semester!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: AHHHHH!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: NOOOOO!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: This is genuinely terrifying!

jokes.text: [High-pitched] I bring you peace.
jokes.setup: Glowing figure approaches again
jokes.punchline: Same gentle alien voice from before
jokes.explanation: Good callback building anticipation for the reveal. The repeated gentle voice maintains mystery.

dialogue.speaker: Alien
dialogue.line: Greetings, Mr. Burns. We have returned.

jokes.text: Uh, Mr. Nimoy, we have 10 minutes left. - Oh. Fine. Well, let me just get... uh, something out of my car. [Footsteps] [Door] [Car driving away] I don't think he's coming back.
jokes.setup: Producer tells Nimoy they have time left to fill
jokes.punchline: Nimoy clearly abandons the show, producer's understated observation
jokes.explanation: Excellent visual/audio gag with perfect deadpan delivery. The progression from excuse to car departure to understatement is beautifully timed.

dialogue.speaker: Producer
dialogue.line: We have forty-five seconds of airtime to fill.

dialogue.speaker: Leonard Nimoy
dialogue.line: How long can you hold your breath?

dialogue.speaker: Producer
dialogue.line: He's gone.

jokes.text: Do Sonny and Cher still have that stupid show? - No. She won an Oscar, and he's a congressman. - Good night!
jokes.setup: Man wakes from 23-year coma asking about Sonny and Cher
jokes.punchline: Upon hearing their divergent career paths, immediately chooses to go back into coma
jokes.explanation: Brilliant concept perfectly executed. The idea that modern reality is worse than a coma is both dark and hilarious.

dialogue.speaker: Coma patient
dialogue.line: What happened to Sonny and Cher?

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Well, they split up years ago. Sonny went into politics and became a congressman. Cher became a solo artist and actress.

dialogue.speaker: Coma patient
dialogue.line: Put me back in the coma.

jokes.text: Local man, Homer Simpson, shown here with his tongue stuck to a lamppost, has given us this videotape.
jokes.setup: News introduces Homer's alien footage
jokes.punchline: Identifies him with embarrassing lamppost incident
jokes.explanation: Good character moment establishing Homer's reputation for embarrassing incidents. The lamppost detail is perfectly specific.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: We've just received footage of what appears to be an alien spacecraft. The man who captured this remarkable video is Homer Simpson, a local nuclear plant worker. Mr. Simpson also made headlines three years ago when he was found naked, hugging a lamppost at 3 AM, singing sea shanties.

jokes.text: It's a close encounter of the blurred kind.
jokes.setup: News describes Homer's alien footage
jokes.punchline: Pun on 'Close Encounters of the Third Kind' referencing poor video quality
jokes.explanation: Decent pun on famous movie title. Works well for news anchor delivery but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: We take you now to what appears to be footage of an alien spacecraft. Or as the video quality suggests, a close encounter of the turd kind.

jokes.text: The entire Channel 6 news team will be there except for Phil, the boom mike operator, who's getting fired tomorrow. [Groans] Very unprofessional, Phil.
jokes.setup: Anchor announces news team coverage
jokes.punchline: Casually announces Phil's firing on air, criticizes his reaction
jokes.explanation: Excellent cringe comedy. The casual workplace cruelty and meta-commentary on boom mike operators is perfectly uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: We have some news to share with our viewers. Phil, you're fired.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: What? But—

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: And frankly, Phil, I'm disappointed in your reaction. A professional would have handled that with more grace.

jokes.text: Is the alien carbon-based or silicon-based? - Uh, the second one. 'Zilliphone.'
jokes.setup: Doctor asks scientific question about alien composition
jokes.punchline: Homer makes up word 'zilliphone' instead of 'silicon'
jokes.explanation: Good Homer malapropism. 'Zilliphone' is a believable corruption of 'silicon' showing his scientific ignorance.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Hibbert
dialogue.line: What element do you think aliens might be composed of?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Zilliphone!

jokes.text: Is the alien Santa Claus? - Uh, yes.
jokes.setup: Ralph asks nonsensical question
jokes.punchline: Homer agrees to absurd suggestion
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Ralph's innocent stupidity and Homer's desperate agreement to any suggestion.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Why do we need a tiger?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's a good point. Let's get one.

jokes.text: I remember another gentle visitor from the heavens. He came in peace, and then died... only to come back to life. And his name was... E.T., the extraterrestrial. [Sniffles] I love that little guy.
jokes.setup: Reverend describes Christ-like figure from heaven
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's talking about E.T., gets emotional about the movie
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection. Sets up expectation of religious reference then subverts with E.T. The emotional delivery sells it perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: And then this heavenly figure descended from above, radiating pure compassion and divine wisdom.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: Of course, I'm talking about E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial.

dialogue.speaker: Reverend Lovejoy
dialogue.line: What a picture. What a picture.

jokes.text: Hey, Spock! What do you want on your hot dog? - Surprise me.
jokes.setup: Vendor calls Nimoy by his character name
jokes.punchline: Nimoy's cool, unflappable response
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Nimoy's professional acceptance of being called Spock. Simple but effective.

dialogue.speaker: Vendor
dialogue.line: Live long and prosper!

dialogue.speaker: Leonard Nimoy
dialogue.line: I shall.

jokes.text: You don't see any 'Homer Is A Dope' T-shirts, do you? - We sold those out in five minutes.
jokes.setup: Homer hopefully looks for supportive merchandise
jokes.punchline: Reveals insulting shirts sold out immediately
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment showing Homer's delusions versus reality. The quick sale of insulting merchandise is believable.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Do you have any supportive shirts? You know, something that says 'I'm with stupid'?

dialogue.speaker: Vendor
dialogue.line: Yeah, we sold out of those in five minutes.

jokes.text: These shirts are 100% cotton. And look at the fine stitching on 'Dope.' - I'll take two.
jokes.setup: Marge tries to find positive aspects of insulting shirt
jokes.punchline: Customer appreciates quality craftsmanship of insult
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge moment trying to be supportive while customer focuses on quality of the insult. Good character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, at least the embroidery is very well done.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: You know, you're right. That is quality craftsmanship.

jokes.text: I bring you love. - Is that the love between a man and a woman or the love of a man for a fine Cuban cigar?
jokes.setup: Burns offers love to humanity
jokes.punchline: Homer asks for specific clarification about type of love
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's specific priorities. The Cuban cigar reference is perfectly random yet logical for him.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: I'm offering my love to all of humanity.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Even to me?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Yes, even to you.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Is it the kind of love that involves physical contact?

jokes.text: It's bringing love! Don't let it get away! - Break its legs!
jokes.setup: Crowd recognizes alien offers love
jokes.punchline: Immediately wants to attack it violently
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark satire of mob mentality. The immediate violent response to offered love is perfectly absurd and disturbing.

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: An alien! It offers its love!

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Get it! Kill it!

jokes.text: It's not a monster. It's Mr. Burns. - Oh, it's Mr. Burns. Kill it! Kill it!
jokes.setup: Smithers reveals alien is actually Mr. Burns
jokes.punchline: Crowd's bloodlust increases upon learning his identity
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation. The reveal that it's Burns makes the crowd MORE violent, not less. Perfect commentary on his unpopularity. Became iconic.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: It's not an alien—it's Mr. Burns!

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Get him! Kill him! Destroy him!

jokes.text: The most rewarding part was when he gave me my money.
jokes.setup: Smithers describes helping Burns through medical ordeal
jokes.punchline: Only cares about getting paid
jokes.explanation: Decent character moment showing Smithers' mercenary side beneath his loyalty. Understated but effective.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: I've been by Mr. Burns' side through every medical procedure, every hospital stay, every moment of suffering and pain.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: And do you know what kept me going? Not the satisfaction of helping my boss recover... but knowing I'd finally get that raise he promised me.

jokes.text: A lifetime of working in a nuclear power plant has given me a healthy green glow... and left me as impotent as a Nevada boxing commissioner.
jokes.setup: Burns explains his glowing appearance
jokes.punchline: Compares impotence to corrupt boxing officials
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor combining radiation effects with sports corruption metaphor. The specificity of 'Nevada boxing commissioner' is perfectly Burns.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: You see, I glow because I'm radioactive. As for my... diminished virility, it's like a corrupt boxing official—everyone can see the fix is in, but nobody does anything about it.

jokes.text: And now that I'm back to normal, I don't bring you peace and love. I bring you fear, famine, pestilence and- Time for a booster.
jokes.setup: Burns reveals his true evil nature
jokes.punchline: Gets cut off mid-threat by medical intervention
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns moment showing his cartoonish evil interrupted by medical necessity. The timing of the interruption is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: You see, I'm going to destroy you all and take over the world with my evil plan to—

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Mr. Burns, you need to take your medication.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: But I was in the middle of my diabolical monologue!

jokes.text: And so concludes our tale. I'm Leonard Nimoy. Good night, and keep watching the skis! Uh, skies.
jokes.setup: Nimoy delivers closing narration
jokes.punchline: Mispronounces 'skies' as 'skis,' corrects himself
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective ending gag. The mispronunciation and correction humanizes the narrator figure.

dialogue.speaker: Leonard Nimoy
dialogue.line: And so the Simpson family gazed up at the beautiful skis... skies. I mean skies.

tmdb_overview: Homer witnesses something out of this world in Springfield woods, but no one believes him -- not even FBI agents Fox Mulder and Dana Scully, who come to investigate the incident.
insight: X-Files parody sustains 3.4 jokes per minute across 22-minute runtime with consistent character work.
